coredata.dc:title: Synthesis, characterization, and application of gene conjugated polymerized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots carriers for in vivo bio-targeting in neuroblastoma treatment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, You-Sheng
Lin, Kuen-Song
Chen, Yun
Mdlovu, Ndumiso Vukile
coredata.dc:description:
Background Neuroblastoma is the most prevalent cancer that affects infants. It arises from neural crest elements of the sympathetic nervous system. Accurate medical diagnostic techniques are required to achieve targeted treatment.
Methods Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized using a classic hydrothermal method. The N-GQDs were further coated with polyethylene glycol (PEG): branched polyethylenimine (b-PEI) to form PEG:b-PEI@N-GQDs (P@N-GQDs). The P@N-GQDs were coupled with an anti-GD2 antibody to form Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs, which were then visualized using an in vivo imaging system (IVIS). The structure, optical properties, and in vivo imaging performance of the fluorescent markers were investigated.
Significant findings The P@N-GQDs had an irregular circular shape with 10.6 ± 1.7 nm diameter. The PL emission of P@N-GQDs was extended from 450 ± 10 to 550 ± 10 nm. The visibility of P@N-GQDs in the IVIS spectrum was also improved, enhancing the signal to 1.83 times that achieved by N-GQDs in vivo. The Ab-GD2-P@N-GQDs accumulated in cancer cells, and the maximum fluorescence intensity in the dissected tumor was 6.72 × 107. The proposed probe successfully targeted neuroblastoma and has great potential as a gene carrier.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Antibody GD2
Targeted therapy
Neuroblastoma
Bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Two-step polymerization of N-GQDs co-coated with b-PEI and PEG (P@N-GQDs)
Anti-GD2 antibody conjugation and pcDNA combination with P@N-GQDs
Transfection of BE(2)-M17 cells by pcDNA-EGFP@P@N-GQDs
In vitro cytocompatibility and cytotoxicity tests of P@N-GQDs (MTT and LDH assays)
Structure characterization and imaging of P@N-GQDs
In vitro cell viability, cytotoxicity, and bioimaging studies
In vivo targeting of neuroblastoma by anti-GD2 antibodies and IVIS imaging

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns and HR-TEM images of (a,c) heat dried and (b,d) freeze dried N-doped graphene quantum dots, (e) number distribution of N-GQDs (1–4); (f) DLS and zeta potential measurement of P@N-GQDs, (g) HR-TEM image of P@N-GQDs and (h) FE-TEM liquid phase image of Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs.

(a) UV-Vis spectra of N-GQDs, the inserted pictures describe the blue emission of green N-GQDs solution (Ex: 365 nm). (b) Band gap analysis of different dopant concentration N-GQDs. (c) The contour plotting of N-GQDs-4, range of Ex: 300‒400 nm and Em: 400‒500 nm, and (d) the comparison of size and UV signal. (e) The UV‒Vis spectra of P@N-GQDs presents the concentration dependent absorbance, and (f) the relationship between concentration and UV-Vis signal. (g) The PL spectra of P@N-GQDs (2 mg/mL) was characterized with excitation 300‒500 nm. (h) The 3-D fluorescence matrix of (g) is presented (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

The organic functional group and elements composition of freeze dried N-GQDs-4; (a) The FT-IR spectra comparison of N-GQDs and GQDs; (b) The polymer layer coating can be seen via FT‒IR. (c) Raman spectra of freeze dried N-GQDs (1–4) analyzed using D/G band, (d) Raman spectra shows the defect peak of polymer layers.

(a) Full scan with C1s, O1s, and N1s orbital obtained from XPS spectra of N-GQDs, detail scanning of (c) N1s (d) C1s, and (e) O1s of N-GQDs-4 show (b) three different forms of N doping. The polymer shell provides signal in C1s, O1s, and N1s analysis of XPS spectra (f–h) with NH3 functional group or carbon skeleton.

MTT assay and LDH assay of (a,b) nitrogen doping N-GQD with different concentration, (c,d) Single polymer coating with N-GQDs, (e,f) different PEG, and b-PEI polymer ratios coating with N-GQDs, (g,h) different amounts of P@N-GQDs (GI 1:1) treating with BE(2)-M17.

The CLSM images of BE(2)M17 cell treated with N-GQDs and P@N-GQDs (GI 1:1), the images were acquired with (a,d) visible, (b,e) merge (c,g) DAPI filter and GFP filter (treated with pcDNA), merge images were used to distinguish the enter of P@N-GQDs with pcDNA protein.

The in vivo IVIS spectra of (a) N-GQDs and P@N-GQDs subcutaneous tissue injection; (b) The yellow liner signal scanning in (a) were displayed with related ratio; (c) The in vivo fluorescence emission (Ex: 535 nm / Em: Cy5.5) of induced tumor organ in nude mice with Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs injection were divided into N-GQDs, P@N-GQDs and Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs (Anti-GQDs), (d) the dissected organs of treated nude mice were detected with IVIS in Ex: 535 nm and Em: Cy5.5. (e) The dissected organs of mice with Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs injection were measured with Ex: 430, 465, 500, 535 nm (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).
The synthesized process of P@N-GQDs and Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Synthesis, characterization, and application of gene conjugated polymerized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots carriers for in vivo bio-targeting in neuroblastoma treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Chung–Li District, Taoyuan City 32003, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Chung–Li District, Taoyuan City 32003, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Surgery, Far Eastern Memorial Hospital, New Taipei City 220, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Surgery, Far Eastern Memorial Hospital, New Taipei City 220, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate Institute of Medicine, Yuan Ze University, Chung–Li District, Taoyuan City 32003, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cGraduate Institute of Medicine, Yuan Ze University, Chung–Li District, Taoyuan City 32003, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Neuroblastoma is the most prevalent cancer that affects infants. It arises from neural crest elements of the sympathetic nervous system. Accurate medical diagnostic techniques are required to achieve targeted treatment.

Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized using a classic hydrothermal method. The N-GQDs were further coated with polyethylene glycol (PEG): branched polyethylenimine (b-PEI) to form PEG:b-PEI@N-GQDs (P@N-GQDs). The P@N-GQDs were coupled with an anti-GD2 antibody to form Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs, which were then visualized using an in vivo imaging system (IVIS). The structure, optical properties, and in vivo imaging performance of the fluorescent markers were investigated.

The P@N-GQDs had an irregular circular shape with 10.6 ± 1.7 nm diameter. The PL emission of P@N-GQDs was extended from 450 ± 10 to 550 ± 10 nm. The visibility of P@N-GQDs in the IVIS spectrum was also improved, enhancing the signal to 1.83 times that achieved by N-GQDs in vivo. The Ab-GD2-P@N-GQDs accumulated in cancer cells, and the maximum fluorescence intensity in the dissected tumor was 6.72 × 107. The proposed probe successfully targeted neuroblastoma and has great potential as a gene carrier.

ce:section.ce:para: Neuroblastoma (NB) is an embryonic tumor of the autonomic nervous system, it is typically found in tissues of the sympathetic nervous system. There are 102,000 cases of neuroblastoma in children under 15 years of age were reported [1,2]. There are 50% late stage (stage IV) extracranial solid tumor are incurable, the metastatic dissemination of NB and poor prognosis also happened frequently [3]. Therefore, it presents a significant diagnostic challenge. NB seldom manifests early and is difficult to detect. It leads to high-risk disease in more than 50% of patients [4] and allows the disease to progress, impairing the resistance of patients to other diseases and infections. Previous studies have shown that GD2 ganglioside is abundantly expressed in the cancer cell surface of neuroectodermal origin. It has a limited distribution in normal cells. Based on this difference, GD2 ganglioside has been determined as an ideal target for the delivery of therapeutic agents [3]. The commercial anti-GD2 products are helpful in promoting the development of diagnostic techniques. Recently, nanotechnology-based cancer therapies and diagnostic techniques have been explored [5–7], but the research focusing on NB targeting is relatively limited [1,2,5].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted considerable research attention in recent years, owing to their size-tunable bandgap, stable photoluminescence [8], low toxicity [9], strong electrochemical properties [10], and desirable chemical properties attributed to their aromatic rings [10,11]. GQDs are zero-dimensional members of the carbon family with unique physical characteristics and a nanoscale size (<20 nm). Because the cytotoxicity of graphene compounds depends on their matrix materials, they have been investigated as biomaterials. According to the previous studies, GQDs used in the biomedical fields, such as in drug delivery [12], cell imaging [13], biosensor [14], and tissue engineering [15] purposes. Wang et al. labeled LnCaP cells in vitro with confocal fluorescence microscope, the carbon dots performed bright green signal after loading within 2 h [13]. The carbon dots synthesized with green process have been also used for multiple applications, such as in vitro and in vivo image system [14]. Moreover, GQDs are more biocompatible and degradable than QDs, promoting their applicability in biomedical technology, and are able to maintain their unique optical and electronic properties [10,11]. Different dopant of GQDs have widely discussion, the intrinsic electronic, luminescence, and reactive properties are modulated with the applications [16,17]. Nitrogen (N) atom is the first dopant of graphene modification, it is commonly applied to form the pyrrolic structure via graphene skeleton inset [18], N-GQDs has low electronegativity (2.04) which induces the p-type conductivity in GQDs structure [19]. Accordingly, Zhao et al. constructed a biosensor on GQDs to detect human immunoglobulin in vitro [20]; Wang et al. successfully developed a fluorescent drug carrier, i.e., folic acid-conjugated GQDs loaded with doxorubicin [21]; and Chen et al. synthesized large-capacity porous silica nanoparticles containing GQDs as photoluminescence (PL) source [22]. Hydrothermal synthesis, which is a bottom-up [23] technique for generating high-purity and superior nanoparticles with uniform size distribution, has been widely applied in GQD producer. It can be seen that the bottom-up process has the advantages of low energy consumption, low environmental damage, and cost-effectiveness, making it a better strategy better than top-down process [24].

Gene engineering has been considered as a new targeted treatment strategy of cancer, one of the most potential material as gene carrier is GQDs. However, nucleic acids (NAs) are unstable in serum and easily digested by endonuclease; these factors limit the amount of NA that reaches target cells [25,26]. In addition, EGFP transfection is known as an index of gene carrying ability, the EGFP efficacy are largely determined by the synthesized carrier. Most importantly, the concepts of bio carriers is that they are capable of being delivered to specific tumor site for the treatment of the tumor. There are critical properties that inform the performance of gene carriers such as surface charge, size, shape, and surface chemistry [27]. The negative charge associated with the phosphate group causes electrical repulsion between NAs and the carrier surface [28]. Nevertheless, the polymer coating techniques provide a solution for NA conjugates. Primary amines on polyethylenimine (b-PEI) were used to covalently link b-PEI to carboxyl-functionalized nanoparticles, generating a robust b-PEI surface that was strongly positively charged, b-PEI is reported to possess outstanding transfection [29]. The positive surface charge of the GQDs facilitates their combination with negatively charged single-strand DNA (cDNA) owing to the attraction associated with opposite electric charges. The π–π stacking mechanism of cDNA and GQDs has been proposed by Chen et al. [30].

The factors of nucleic acids (NAs) and polymer layer coating have been reported with nanoparticle cross-linking, and the hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) strategy is highly effective for the cross-linking of drug and polymer networks [31]. The size of nanoparticles can be adjusted under different conditions for their biomedical application [32], after considering the difference in the penetrability of the membranes of different cells. In addition, the index of size control includes temperature and solvent polarity; the size distribution demonstrates the nanostructure changes of the nanoparticle system [33].

The Cancers should be targeted using biomarkers, instead of artificial chemicals, to limit the risk of complications. Similarly, the cell image and nanocarrier properties are also important to the in vivo image result, the optical instrument should be used to understand the excitation and emission wavelength of the nanocarrier as well [34]. Accordingly, we designed a biocompatible fluorescent gene nanocarrier to target NB cells in vivo. The dispersal ability of nanoparticles is considered a critical property of gene carriers. We investigated the effects of various synthesis conditions and processes, such as heat drying, freeze-drying, and polymer coating, on the properties of N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) to optimize their particle structure and dispersal ability. In addition, we evaluated the in vitro and in vivo performance of polymer-modified N-GQDs conjugated with anti-GD2 antibodies. These carriers may inform the development of commercial and clinical gene therapy strategies for cancer treatment.
All the materials used in this experiment have been attached in the Supplement section.
The method of N-GQDs synthesis has been attached in the Supplement section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Two-step polymerization of N-GQDs co-coated with b-PEI and PEG (P@N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The detail process has been attached in Supplement. The product particles were designed to consist of N-GQDs co-coated with PEG and b-PEI at a PEG:b-PEI molar ratio of 1:1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-GD2 antibody conjugation and pcDNA combination with P@N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Anti-GD2 pretreatment and conjugation of Shen et al. was modified in the study [35]. The synthesized process of anti-GD2 coating with nanoparticles in this study has been demonstrated in Scheme 1, and the details have been attached in Supplement. Briefly, the pcDNA solution (1 μg/μL) or EDC/NHS activity Anti-GD2 (2 μg/μL) were conjugated with P@N-GQDs (2 μg/μL) and storage at 4 °C overnight to obtain the mixture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transfection of BE(2)-M17 cells by pcDNA-EGFP@P@N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The pcDNA transfection process has been attached in Supplement section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytocompatibility and cytotoxicity tests of P@N-GQDs (MTT and LDH assays)
ce:section.ce:para: A 24-well culture plate was prepared by filling each well with 500 μL DMEM medium and 5 × 103 cells treated with 1% GQD samples (5 μL). Cells incubated for 24 and 48 h at 37 °C under 20% CO2, the MTT and LDH protocols method could be found in Fig. A1 and B1. The biological crystal concentrations were investigated by spectrophotometry of FEMH to evaluate the cytocompatibility and cytotoxicity of the P@N-GQDs. Statistical analysis was performed in triplicate (n = 3).

The animal test (approval No.: IACUC-2019(2)-FYZ-01) was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the Far Eastern Memorial Hospital (FEMH), New Taipei City, Taiwan. The operation was conducted following the guidelines of the Laboratory Animal Center of the FEMH. The nude mice weighing 15‒20 g were provided food and water ad libitum on a 12:12 h day-night cycle (lights on from 6:00 am to 6:00 pm.), with room temperature maintained at approximately 20 °C for four weeks. The subcutaneous tumor was induced by injecting 5 × 105 BE(2)-M17 cells (in 100 μL of PBS) into the right flank of each mouse. The health of the mice and tumor growth was monitored twice per week. The anti-GD2/GQDs (0.4 mg in 100 μL PBS) were injected into the tail vein of anesthetized nude mice until the tumor reached 300 mm3 in volume. The total injection volume of 200 μL was divided into several doses (n = 3–5), minimizing the potency of the composite in vulnerable nude mice. Injected nude mice were imaged with excitation at 430, 465, 500, and 535 nm and emission with Cy5.5. The organs of the mice were harvested after image acquisition, including the lungs, kidneys, liver, pancreas, and heart.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure characterization and imaging of P@N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen dopant affects the crystallinity of the N-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 1 (a,b). After freeze-drying, multiple layers of graphitic (002) peak at 2θ = 22.7‒27.4° were observed, which are ascribed to the disordered structures of carbonyl and nitrogen-bearing functional groups formed in the basal plane [36,37]. The heat drying process allowed for the combination of multiple graphene layers, which led to the formation of broader peaks than those formed due to the freeze-drying method for N-GQDs.

Moreover, the HR-TEM images of the heat-dried and freeze-dried N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 1(c,d), which show that the freeze-dried N-GQDs are better dispersed than the heat-dried N-GQDs. Also, the sizes of heat-dried and freeze-dried N-GQDs are 4.53 ± 0.4 and 2.48 ± 0.38 nm, respectively. The lattice structure (d = 1.29 Å) of N-GQDs was obtained by fast Fourier transform (FFT) calculation in the Gatan DigitalMicrograph analysis program. The aggregation of multiple layers in Fig. 1(c) shows that the heat drying process produces large particles with inconsistent diameters consisting of multiple layers of N-GQDs. Based on these results, we believe that the freeze-dried N-GQDs have a small size and are better dispersed, but the drying process does not have a significant effect on the lattice structure.

DLS analysis was conducted to investigate the number and size distributions of the freeze-dried N-GQDs, and the average size was determined by Gaussian fitting. The number distributions of freeze-dried N-GQDs with different amounts of N dopants are shown in Fig. 1(e). It can be seen that the average size of all N-doped N-GQDs is <5 nm. Moreover, the amount of N dopant was negatively correlated with the N-GQD diameter. The N dopant affects the dipole moment of the GQD networks, resulting in non-uniform electron cloud densities and repulsion between these networks [38]. Based on this previous study, the smaller atom size is associated with the N-GQDs with a minimum diameter of 1.57 nm, which is the smallest size of the freeze-dried N-GQDs. Based on earlier results, the smallest size distribution of freeze-dried N-GQDs-4 was investigated and considered to have the optimal size for serving as a nanocarrier. Based on this concept, freeze-dried N-GQD-4 was identified as the most suitable carrier with controllable size. Therefore, the freeze-dried N-GQD denoted as the “N-GQDs” was used for subsequent studies and analysis.

To prevent the aggregation or cross-linking of PEG and b-PEI polymers during the coating of N-GQDs, a two-step hydrothermal method was used. The ratio of polymerized N-GQDs-4, denoted as GI (PEG: b-PEI), was tested with GI 1:1, GI 1:2, and GI 2:1 to identify the polymer layer effect in Fig. 1(f) with their number distributions. The bio-affinity polymer, PEG, imparts biomedical application potential, increased the size of the aggregated particles, and induces a negative charge after the hydrothermal treatment [39]. Moreover, the zeta potential of PEG- or b-PEI-coated N-GQDs was measured. It can be seen that the large cluster of PEG-coated N-GQDs in Fig. S1(a) has a size of > 1000 nm and the negative charge is measured to be −20.34 ± 0.17 mV (Fig. S1(b)). Although bare b-PEI had a positive charge, which can facilitate size control and achieve distribution of 1.23 nm particles, as shown in Fig. S1(a), the negative charge imparted after the hydrothermal treatment could not be avoided (Fig. S1(b)). However, the matrix of PEG and b-PEI maintained two advantages with the two-step hydrothermal process. The zeta potential measurement and size distribution analysis of P@N-GQDs GI 1:1 revealed a positive surface charge (7.35 mV) and an average diameter of 34.39 ± 2.45 nm. The difference between DLS and HR-TEM results was due to polymer micelle aggregation.

Nevertheless, it can be seen that the aggregation of larger particles was due to the presence of stacked multi-layer GQDs. Furthermore, the polymer coating caused an amorphous peak with high intensity, as shown in Fig. S2, which is attributed to the amorphous phase patterns of the polymerized N-GQDs (P@N-GQDs). The HR-TEM image in Fig. 1(g) of P@N-GQDs shows a spherical particle that is 9.59 nm in diameter with a single-atom array lattice spacing of 1.05 Å, obtained through the DigitalMicrograph program. Moreover, the dispersed anti-GD2 antibody-conjugated P@N-GQDs (Anti-GD2@P@N-GQDs) were characterized by their shape and size in the solution phase. Liquid FE-TEM was used to confirm the dispersion of the Anti-GD2@P@N-GQDs (Fig. 1(h)). Unlike classic TEM, liquid FE-TEM provides more accurate size distribution results, which corrects the aggregation error of the DLS analysis. The single anti-GD2 antibody-conjugated P@N-GQD particles have a diameter of 9–13 nm, which is three times that of unmodified N-GQD particles (2–5 nm). This indicates that the successful application of the antibody coating increased the diameter of the P@N-GQDs by a few nanometers.

The UV–Vis spectra revealed the wavelengths of the N-GQDs with different ratios of the N dopant (Fig. 2 (a)). The strongest UV absorption by the N-GQDs occurred at 270 ± 30 and 350 ± 20 nm, suggesting that the fluorescence of the N-GQDs is associated with π–π* and n–π* transitions, indicating the presence of C=C and C=O bonds, respectively [40]. The quantum yield of N-GQDs was acquired with Quine sulfate standard [41], different N dopant ratios are 4.0, 5.2, 1.3, and 3.5%, respectively. In previous research, the N-GQDs was found that the obvious wide absorbance from 500 to 700 nm, the chromophores organic groups of N-GQDs are similar to methyl blue dye or methyl blue (638.8 nm) dye [42–44] and they correspond to the color, which can be seen under visible light emission. Therefore, the insert figure of Fig. 2(a) shows the green-blue color, which is possibly the color of N-GQDs (aq). In addition, it can be seen that the size-dependent UV–Vis absorption of N-GQDs is due to the quantum size and quantum confinement effect [45] arising from the optical difference of N-GQDs. The band gap evaluation explains the jumping of the electron from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and Fig. 2(b) shows that the energy of the N-GQDs is similar (3.10 ± 0.4 eV) but larger than that of the GQDs (2.84 eV). It is possible that N doping leads to a widening of the band gap, which results in the decrease of the HOMO energy level due to the occupation of the π orbitals by pyridinic nitrogen [46].

The PL spectra reveal the optimal absorbance and fluorescence emission wavelengths of the N-GQDs with different ratios of the N dopant. From the UV–Vis spectra, the PL spectra were acquired from an absorbance range of 300–400 nm and emission of 400–500 nm (Fig. S3(a‒d) [47]). In addition, the contour plot in Fig. 2(c) displays the 2D/3D mapping excitation and emission of N-GQDs-4. The emission at 450 nm owing to the highest intensity has been compared with those at multiple absorbances in Fig. 2(d), and it can be seen that the absorbance at 370 nm has the observed intensity and that for N-GQDs-2 is more prominent than the others. It was found that the decrease in the HOMO level not only widened the band gap but also blocked the amount of excited electrons jumping, which led to the lower PL intensity of N-GQDs-3 [46]. In the comparison of previous results, the quantum confinement effect is associated with the size distribution and UV absorbance, shown in the inset plot of Fig. 2(d), with the strongest UV absorbance observed for 2.56 nm diameter. The size of the N-GQDs may be an effective approach to resolve the issue of the Bohr-radius-limited bulk absorbance [48], and the optimal properties lead to the formation of an unstable state. Although the dimensionality of the N-GQDs is a subject of debate, the Raman spectrum of the freeze-dried N-GQDs should be similar to that of graphene. The UV–Vis spectra provide information on the specific excitation wavelengths of the P@N-GQDs (Fig. 2(e)). The optimal concentration of the P@N-GQDs for their application in bioimaging was determined by measuring the UV absorbance at 270 and 340 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(f). An analysis of the PL properties of the PEG@N-GQDs, b-PEI@N-GQDs, and P@N-GQDs (Fig. 2(g)) revealed that the strongest emission wavelength of the N-GQDs was red-shifted from 450 to 550 nm after polymer modification, because the electron–hole pairs of polymers b-PEI and PEG modify their photoproperties [49]. Fig. S4(a,b) show that the emission of the PEG@N-GQDs ranges from 300 to 500 nm, and the significant shift of the peaks explains the optical modification by the functional group of the polymer. However, the absorbance by the b-PEI layer overlapped that of the N-GQDs but was recovered at 460–500 nm, thus retaining the properties of the N-GQDs. In addition, the PL contour plot of the P@N-GQDs confirms the mapping of absorbance and emission (Fig. 2(h)), and multiple optical properties can be observed. Furthermore, the low energy excitation (> 430 nm) and wide emission wavelength range (440–500 nm) emphasize the suitability of the P@N-GQDs for IVIS imaging (minimum absorbance wavelength ≥ 430 nm).

The FT–IR spectra in Fig. 3 (a) reveal the effects of the carbonyl functional group and N dopant of freeze-dried N-GQDs-4. These effects can be exploited to understand the differences in the GQD and doped ones. The absorbance peaks associated with C–O, and C–C bonds were observed at 1200 cm−1, which indicated the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups [24,50]. Moreover, a peak with a shoulder corresponding to the C=O and C=N bonds is observed at 1640 and 1590 cm−1, which correspond to the skeletal vibration of carbon aromatic rings [19,51]. It can be seen that the increase in the absorbance of the peaks corresponding to C=N and C–N bending at 1394 and 1640 cm−1 reveals the success of N doping. In addition, the peaks at 3200 and 3400 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibration of sp2 C‒H/C=C‒H in aromatic rings, and the shoulder peak associated with –NH vibration possibly appears because of the aromatic rings of the N dopant [52]. The FT–IR spectrum of the polymer coating (Fig. 3(b)) shows peaks at 1150 and 1481, and 1750 (double peak) and 2600 cm−1 attributed to PEG and bPEI. The peaks associated with the characteristic C=N stretching vibration and –NH2 bending vibration of b-PEI are observed at 1285 and 1619 cm−1, respectively. These results confirmed the presence of b-PEI in the coating. The N dopant affects the electron cloud density of the neighboring carbon atoms, thus altering their chemical properties [53].

The Raman spectra of freeze-dried N-GQDs with different dopant ratios reveal disordered D (1350 cm−1) and crystalline G (1580 cm−1) bands, as shown in Fig. 3(c), which correspond to the defects of the doped structure having sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in the graphene network. The intensity ratio of the D/G band in the Raman spectra was determined to estimate the crystallinity of the freeze-dried N-GQDs via the disorder/order structure ratio [54]. Owing to the ultra-small size of the GQDs, the Raman signals experience significant noise and instability. The ID/IG ratios of the freeze-dried N-GQDs range from 0.998 to 2.413, which is larger than that of commercial graphene (ID/IG ≤ 0.5) [55]. The molar ratio of the N dopant increases the defect ratios of the graphitic structure, and the uniform graphene network was transformed by the different electronegativities (C atom> N atom). It has been reported that nitrogen (N) atoms in the lattice might lead to the disappearance of the G band or decrease in the G band intensity in the Raman spectra [56]; freeze-dried N-GQDs-4 also showed similar results. The Raman spectral results of the P@N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 3(d); the graphene and graphene defect peak intensities are lower than those obtained for the N-GQDs. The polymer-coated materials have similar peaks at low Raman shift values in the inset spectra of Fig. 3(d); the bands at 800 cm−1 (787 cm−1) and peaks at 693 and 424 cm−1 are attributed to the skeletal vibrations and C–C–O bending of PEG, respectively [57].

Based on the full-scan XPS spectra ( Fig. 4 (a)) of freeze-dried N-GQDs-4, three elements were considered in the surface bonding analysis: C1s (282‒290 eV), N1s (397‒405 eV), and O1s (531‒536). The XPS spectra in Fig. 4(b) were used to inspect the doping modes of N in the N-GQD structure, and the deconvoluted peaks in the N1s XP spectrum indicated the presence of different types of N bonds, which correspond to pyridinic N (398.5 eV), pyrrolic N (399.1 eV), and graphitic N (401.1 eV) (Fig. 4(c)) [58]. Pyrrolic N is the main component of the C‒N configuration and is detected on the basis of the presence of neighboring carboxyl and amide groups of the graphitic network [36]. Therefore, the C atoms corresponding to the N dopant were investigated through C1s XPS scanning, as shown in Fig. 4(d). The sp2 and sp3 structures of the graphitic network are decorated by the vibration of the aromatic ring, with four peaks corresponding to graphitic carbon (sp2 / sp3, C‒C / C=C), carbonyl (C‒C / C‒O/ C‒N), and carboxylate functional groups (C‒O / COOH). The O1s scanning of the XP spectra (Fig. 4(e)) reveals three peaks: aromatic carbon (C‒O=C / C=O) at 531.2 and 532.7 eV demonstrated the absorbance of resonance from the ring structure, and the functional groups of the graphitic surface (C‒OH) can be seen at 533.2 eV. The XP spectra in Fig. 4(f) show the graphitic bonding in C1 s scanning, where the C‒C bonding ratio increased with the polymer skeleton, which occupied the highest ratios of the P@N-GQDs. The N1s XP spectrum (Fig. 4(g)) of the P@N-GQDs shows the main N-bonding formats associated with graphene rings, namely, quaternary graphitic, pyrrolic, and pyridinic N (graphene ring –N and –NO) [59,60]. Moreover, a significant amount of –NH2 from b-PEI was present on the graphene rings (Fig. 4(h)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell viability, cytotoxicity, and bioimaging studies
ce:section.ce:para: BE(2)-M17 tumor cells were used in the in vitro toxicity test of the fluorescent materials; the volume (μL) ratio of medium to fluorescent material was 500:5. BE(2)-M17 cells were treated with N-GQDs to evaluate their cytotoxicity. To our knowledge, the good bio-affinity of materials is due to the high cell viability (> 80%) and low cytotoxicity (< 10%). The results of cell viability (MTT) and cytotoxicity (LDH) in Fig. 5 (a,b) indicate that the cell viability of the treated cells exceeded 90% and that the cytotoxicity of N-GQDs in vitro applications was low (less than 5%). The results confirmed the inverse relationship between the amount of nitrogen dopant and cell viability. As shown in Fig. 5(c,d), the cell viability and cytotoxicity of PEG or b-PEI and their coating layers can be analyzed, as cell death leads to positive charges of bare b-PEI (MTT<10% and LDH>50%). However, the hydrothermal process simultaneously reduces surface charge and toxicity at the same time. Furthermore, the P@N-GQDs (Fig. 5(e,f)) caused limited damage to the cells (<80%). Cell viability and cytotoxicity were relatively independent of the PEG:b-PEI ratio; however, the P@N-GQDs with a PEG:b-PEI ratio of 1:1 achieved the most stable results.

The effect of the treatment volume on cytotoxicity was investigated; Fig. 5(g,h) shows the results of cells treated with 5, 10, and 15 μL of P@N-GQDs. The cell viability after 48 h (at treatment volumes exceeding 10 μL) was greater than that after 24 h, suggesting that cell division was accelerated by higher volumes of P@N-GQDs. This increase may be explained by the biocompatibility of the polymer, which enhances cell growth. The cytotoxicity approaching zero also illustrates the nontoxic nature of the material. In conclusion, at low concentrations (< 2% of medium volume), P@N-GQDs with a PEG:b-PEI ratio of 1:1 showed stable and low cytotoxicity toward BE(2)-M17 cells.

The bio-imaging tests were divided into two parts, as shown in Fig. 6 (a‒g). First, the fluorescence of the treated cells was investigated using CLSM. The DAPI filtered fluorescence (Fig. 6(c,g)) and bright images (Fig. 6(a,d)) of the treated cells reflect similar shapes (Fig. 6(b,e)) owing to the penetration of the cell membranes by the N-GQDs and P@N-GQDs. Moreover, the expression of P@N-GQDs was higher in the treated cells because of the biocompatibility of the polymer coating attached to the cell membrane. The transfection of BE(2)-M17 cells was subsequently performed to determine the NA-carrying ability of P@N-GQDs (Fig. 6(f)), and pcDNA3-EGFP was added to 10 μg of P@N-GQDs to obtain pcDNA3-EGFP@P@N-GQDs that were used to treat the BE(2)-M17 cells. After treatment with an inhibitor for 24 h, BE(2)-M17 cells expressed EGFPs with strong fluorescence. The stacking of blue fluorescence and bright images can be explained by the success of nanoparticles, and the emission of EGFP and DAPI indicates the signal from GQD and pcDNA3-EGFP. This experiment is a prerequisite for gene-carrying analyses. According to the literature, pcDNA3-EGFP is widely used in transfection experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo targeting of neuroblastoma by anti-GD2 antibodies and IVIS imaging
ce:section.ce:para:
BE(2)-M17 cancer cells were transplanted into the subcutaneous tissues of nude mice. The IVIS spectra were recorded 30 min after the sample injection (injected volume: 200 μL). Various absorbance wavelengths were used to achieve a suitable image resolution (430, 465, 500, and 535 nm). The N-GQDs and P@N-GQDs were excited at 430–465 nm and 430–535 nm, respectively. In comparison with similar excitation/emission conditions, N-GQDs and P@N-GQDs injected into the subcutaneous tissue of the animal model (Fig. 7
(a)) exhibit high photon intensity under an excitation wavelength of 465 nm using a GFP filter (515–575 nm). In terms of radiant efficiency (Fig. 7(b)), the intensity of the photons emitted by the P@N-GQDs was 1.83 times greater than that of the photons emitted by the N-GQDs. P@N-GQDs have been demonstrated to have a higher IVIS signal performance than bare N-GQDs, and this effect is attributed to the multi-emission properties of the polymer coating.

The tumor targeting of the anti-GD2 antibody (Ab-GD2)-coated P@N-GQDs (Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs) is shown in Fig. 7(c,d). To circumvent the physiological responses associated with surgery, 200 μL of Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs was injected through the tail vein of a nude mouse, 50 μL at a time. BE(2)-M17 tumor cells were transplanted into animal models to induce tumor growth. After tumorigenesis, N-GQDs, P@N-GQDs, and Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs were administered. The optimum conditions for IVIS imaging (excitation wavelength: 535 nm; excitation filter: Cy5.5) were determined to obtain high-resolution images (Fig. 7(c)). The tumor was emphasized by the fluorescence of the accumulated Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs. In addition, the harvested organs were neatly arranged to illustrate the different fluorescence intensities observed in the organs and tumors.

Tumor targeting has been challenged due to its lack of secondary organ specificity, which is considered that the nanocarrier might be misdirected to the excretory organs [61], such as kidney and liver. Therefore, the related fluorescence intensity of tumor and others is applied to understand the pathway and accumulation of P@N-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 7(d), the accumulated Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs produced strong fluorescence (Max. = 6.72 × 107) in the neuroblastoma tumor, which is consistent with the emission observed in Fig. 7(e) associated with Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs. Moreover, the excitation wavelength range was increased to achieve multi-wavelength excitation/fixed-wavelength emission PL, as shown in Fig. 2(g). Different excitation wavelengths (435, 465, 500, and 535 nm) were applied to evaluate the PL in tumor cells of harvested organs, as shown in Fig. 7(e). IVIS imaging can identify the tissue in the body targeted by the Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs. Furthermore, the application of different filters can distinguish the accumulation of P@N-GQDs in various organs. The results further demonstrate the best absorption of multiple excitation wavelengths (535 nm) by the Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs accumulated in tumor tissue in vivo. Mostly, biological effects of nanocarriers are mainly associated with their uptake ability. The size, rigidity, charge, shape, protein composition, and type of cells are fundamental criterion to be taken into consideration for the mechanism of nanocarriers into cells. The penetration of the nanocarriers into the cells is the first step towards an effective delivery process. Nanoparticles can be administered into the body through oral ingestion, ocular penetration, and injection. In the current study, the Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs may be administered intravenously through injection. The proposed main mechanism of cellular uptake is the clathrin-mediated endocytosis, which is the cellular entry mechanism to internalize specific molecules such as nanocarriers into the cells. After the nanocarriers have penetrated the cells, most of the nanocarriers are accumulated in lysosome. Successful PEI-mediated gene delivery is dependent on both clathrin- and caveolin-dependent pathways. Previous studies have shown that PEGs can act as P-glycoprotein (P-gp) inhibitors with the potential to affect the absorption and efflux of concomitantly administered drugs. In addition, nanocarriers with particles sizes ranging between 30 and 50 nm interacts efficiently with clathrin-mediated receptors and are successively internalized through receptor-mediated endocytosis [62,63].

This study explored the anti-GD2 conjugated bi-polymer-coated PEG-b-PEI@N-GQDs (P@N-GQDs) via a two-step hydrothermal process for accurate probing of NB tumor cells in vivo. An increase in the nitrogen doping amount affected the single-atom layer (d = 0.12 nm), with N-GQD size distribution from 3.7 to 1.6 nm. The Raman signal (ID/IG ratio = 0.998) and peaks associated with the pyridinic N bond and C=N stretching demonstrated that doping with nitrogen was successful. The liquid phase electron microscope indicated a polymer layer stacking of the P@N-GQDs with a diameter of 9.59 nm and number distribution of 32.36 nm. The shifted UV–Vis absorption and PL emission wavelengths of the P@N-GQDs were 440 and 550 nm, respectively, which provided good resolution in the Ex: 550 nm and Em: Cy 5.5 of the IVIS spectrum. The cytocompatibility and cytotoxicity of the nanocarriers indicate their suitability for application in biological fields. In addition, cell transfection of P@N-GQDs coupled with EGFPs resulted in enhanced fluorescence. IVIS imaging of Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs successfully tracked cancer cells in vivo. The in vivo IVIS signal linear scanning of P@N-GQDs was 1.832 times more than that of N-GQDs, rendering P@N-GQDs a more suitable bioimaging nanocarrier in subcutaneous tissue. The Ab-GD2 conjugated P@N-GQDs indicated excitation (500 nm) and emission (Cy5.5), which are the best conditions for acquiring the signal accumulation of markers in induced NB tumors. In conclusion, the Ab-GD2@P@N-GQDs successfully targeted a neuroblastoma tumor in vivo and have great potential as gene carriers.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial fund support of the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST), Taiwan, (MOST107–2221-E-155–001-MY3) is gratefully acknowledged. We also deeply thank Dr. Ya-Hui Tsai of FEMH for the helps in providing professional advice on bio-test and experimental design suggestion. Thanks to Mr. Mao-Jung Huang and Chia-Ying Chien of Ministry of Science and Technology (National Taiwan University) for the assistance in FE-TEM experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2021.104167.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found in the online version.
